/autoresearch Goal: Improve the advisor binary-gate model with measurable real-world gain and low-risk promotion. Context: - Current released control is advisor-0.1.9 with improved local routing performance. - Keep behavior unchanged (no runtime logic changes), only model/eval artifact updates. - Preserve loop-owned check-in flow and existing thresholds unless evidence justifies a change. Task: Run a focused model-improvement cycle with hard risk controls and explicit promotion gates. Execution plan: 1) Baseline + data refresh - Run the standard training pipeline end-to-end (fresh run): - routing:mine - routing:queue -- --per-label 50 --ambiguous 150 - routing:autolabel - binary:build - Capture dataset stats and keep artifacts in `data/routing/` (ignored by git where relevant). 2) Build targeted hard-eval suites before touching promotion - Extract and label a compact hard set (100–200 rows max): - auto-label low-confidence rows, - model-vs-heuristic disagreements, - conflict rows / policy-tension cases. - Add at least one external benchmark holdout (e.g., terminal-bench small mapped to continue/escalate) with dedicated report. 3) Train candidate variants - Train the baseline candidate once from current data. - Run at least one small variation (threshold or feature/candidate source weighting variation) for comparison. - Track each variant with: - binary:train - binary:eval-candidates - binary:eval-sources - binary:eval-file on: - data/routing/binary-gate.jsonl - external benchmark holdout - conflict/hard slice holdout 4) Decision gate (promotion rules) Promote only if: - Local routing full eval is not worse than current best baseline (accuracy/F1 not materially down). - External benchmark slice is >= baseline or within non-regression tolerance. - Conflict slice error is reduced or unchanged (no new critical conflict regressions). - Golden/control behavior on operationally risky cases is stable (no policy-regression in top misses). - Candidate passes `npm run check && npm run test`. 5) If promoting - Sync artifact to: - `/Users/pavel/.pi/agent/fiale-plus/advisor/binary-gate-model.json` - `packages/advisor/assets/binary-gate-model.json` - Produce one PR-ready summary: - before/after metrics table, - top misses with short cause analysis, - explicit go/no-go + rollback plan. Constraints: - No PR/merge/release in this cycle unless explicitly requested. - Keep all generated evidence files under `data/routing/` or `/tmp/`. - Do not touch behavior code paths (advisor/orchestration runtime logic).

merged, now on the ticketd to integrate llm to advisor - see feedback I’d **adapt most of Grok’s feedback**, but reject one part: don’t make the first PR too “orchestration-bus ambitious.” The winning MVP is still **inside advisor**, not a generic cross-package conductor yet. What I’d keep: * **Policy object, not category-only**. * **Local-first classifier as Phase 2**, not required for first merge. * **Deterministic safety override** before model output. * **Logging/evals from day one**. * **Session config remains authoritative**: `mode` and `review` constrain/router output, not replaced by it. * **Delegate hints** are useful, but should be optional metadata in MVP, not hard integration. What I’d reject or postpone: * Sub-100ms as a hard acceptance criterion. Nice target, but not required for first implementation. * Fine-tuning the classifier early. First collect routing logs and evals. * Parallel direct advisor side-channel. Cool, but likely scope creep for Issue #7. Here’s the payload I’d hand to the harness to update the ticket. ````md # Update Issue #7 Rename the issue to: **Advisor Intent Router: per-turn policy for preflight, review, and direct advice** Replace the current description with the following: --- ## Problem `pi-advisor` already has the right primitives: - `mode: "auto" | "manual" | "off"` - `review: "light" | "strict" | "off"` - `before_agent_start` preflight injection - `turn_end` / `agent_end` review - `/advisor` manual command - brief/cache-aware session state - model resolution / SOTA fallback But current advisor behavior is too uniform. A trivial edit, a debugging task, an architecture decision, a review request, and a direct strategic question all pass through roughly the same static preflight mechanism. This creates two problems: 1. advisor noise on simple turns 2. not enough intentionality on high-risk / high-value turns The goal is not to bolt on a new subsystem. The goal is to make the existing advisor adaptive per turn. ## Proposal Add an **Advisor Intent Router** inside `packages/advisor`. On `before_agent_start`, classify the latest user turn into a compact per-turn policy. That policy decides: - whether to inject preflight - whether preflight should be light or full - whether to route directly to advisor - what post-review behavior should happen - whether safety-sensitive handling should be triggered - whether repo-memory / brain / repo-arch context may be useful This should preserve existing config compatibility. Session-level config remains authoritative; the router only adapts behavior within those boundaries. ## Policy shape ```ts export type AdvisorIntent = | "quick_edit" | "implementation" | "debug" | "architecture" | "review" | "question" | "safety_sensitive" | "repo_memory"; export type PreflightPolicy = "off" | "light" | "full" | "direct"; export type ReviewPolicy = "off" | "light" | "strict" | "error_only"; export interface AdvisorTurnPolicy { intent: AdvisorIntent; preflight: PreflightPolicy; review: ReviewPolicy; directAdvisor: boolean; safety: boolean; repoMemory: boolean; /** * Optional future-facing delegation hints. * MVP may log these without executing delegation yet. */ delegate?: Array<"guardrails" | "brain" | "repo-arch">; confidence: number; reason: string; } ```` ## Routing table | Intent | Preflight | Review | Notes | | ------------------ | --------------- | ------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | | `quick_edit` | `off` | `off` | confirmations, formatting, tiny edits, show diff, continue | | `implementation` | `light` | `light` | normal coding task | | `debug` | `off` / `light` | `error_only` | avoid generic planning; focus on failures | | `architecture` | `full` | `strict` | design, refactor, API shape, framework choice, tradeoffs | | `review` | `off` | `strict` | user asks to assess/check/review existing work | | `question` | `direct` | `off` | route directly to advisor without forcing a normal agent turn | | `safety_sensitive` | `full` | `strict` | destructive commands, auth, secrets, migrations, production config | | `repo_memory` | `light` | `light` | repo-history / “why was this done?” / conventions / prior decisions | ## Config interaction Existing config must continue to work. Suggested behavior: ```ts function applySessionConfig( policy: AdvisorTurnPolicy, config: AdvisorConfig ): AdvisorTurnPolicy { if (config.mode === "off") { return { ...policy, preflight: "off", review: "off", directAdvisor: false, }; } if (config.mode === "manual") { return { ...policy, preflight: "off", directAdvisor: false, }; } if (config.review === "off") { policy.review = "off"; } if (config.review === "strict" && policy.review !== "off") { policy.review = "strict"; } return policy; } ``` Session-level `review: "strict"` should act as a multiplier. It can increase review strength, but should not force review for obvious `quick_edit` unless we intentionally choose that later. ## Deterministic safety override Before trusting the classifier, run cheap deterministic checks for dangerous patterns. Examples: * deleting files/directories * migrations * auth/security changes * secrets/env files * production config * dependency upgrades * network calls / publishing / deployment * permission changes * destructive shell commands If safety rules match, override the policy: ```ts if (looksSafetySensitive(event)) { policy = { intent: "safety_sensitive", preflight: "full", review: "strict", directAdvisor: false, safety: true, repoMemory: false, delegate: ["guardrails"], confidence: 1, reason: "deterministic safety-sensitive pattern matched", }; } ``` False positives are acceptable here. False negatives are not. ## Fallback policy Classifier failure must never break advisor flow. If classification fails or confidence is low, use conservative light behavior: ```ts const FALLBACK_POLICY: AdvisorTurnPolicy = { intent: "implementation", preflight: "light", review: "light", directAdvisor: false, safety: false, repoMemory: false, confidence: 0, reason: "fallback policy", }; ``` Recommended confidence behavior: ```ts if (policy.confidence < 0.55) { policy = { ...FALLBACK_POLICY, reason: `low classifier confidence: ${policy.reason}`, }; } ``` ## Implementation sketch Inside `registerAdvisor`, add a policy classification step before current static preflight injection. ```ts pi.on("before_agent_start", async (event, ctx) => { if (config.mode === "off") { return { systemPrompt: event.systemPrompt }; } let policy = await classifyAdvisorPolicy({ prompt: event.prompt, brief: brief(loadState()), proposedAction: event.proposedAction, }, ctx); policy = applyDeterministicOverrides(policy, event); policy = applySessionConfig(policy, config); saveLastPolicy(policy); logPolicyDecision(policy, event); if (policy.preflight === "off") { return { systemPrompt: event.systemPrompt }; } if (policy.preflight === "direct") { const answer = await askAdvisor(pi, ctx, event.prompt, policy.intent, true); ctx.ui?.notify?.(answer.text, "info"); return { systemPrompt: event.systemPrompt }; } return { systemPrompt: [ event.systemPrompt, renderPolicyPreflight(policy), brief(loadState()) ? `Brief:
${brief(loadState())}` : "", ].filter(Boolean).join("

"), }; }); ``` Then make `turn_end` / `agent_end` read the saved policy: ```ts const policy = loadLastPolicy(); if (policy.review === "off") return; if (policy.review === "error_only" && !failed) return; if (policy.review === "light" && (fileChanged || failed)) { runReview("light"); } if (policy.review === "strict") { runReview("strict"); } ``` ## Classifier prompt Initial classifier can use the existing model resolution path. Later we can move it to a dedicated local tiny model. Prompt: ```text You are the intent router for a coding-agent advisor. Classify the latest user turn into an advisor policy. The advisor should be quiet for trivial edits and confirmations. The advisor should be stronger for architecture, refactors, risky changes, security, migrations, dependency upgrades, and ambiguous goals. Debugging usually needs error-focused review more than generic preflight. Review requests need strict review, not planning. Direct strategic questions should route directly to advisor. Repo-history or "why was this done?" questions may need repo memory. Return strict JSON only. Schema: { "intent": "quick_edit | implementation | debug | architecture | review | question | safety_sensitive | repo_memory", "preflight": "off | light | full | direct", "review": "off | light | strict | error_only", "directAdvisor": true, "safety": false, "repoMemory": false, "delegate": ["guardrails", "brain", "repo-arch"], "confidence": 0.0, "reason": "short reason" } User turn: {{prompt}} Recent advisor brief: {{brief}} Proposed action, if available: {{proposedAction}} ``` ## Model strategy Phase 1: Use the existing advisor model resolution path. Keep the classifier cheap by limiting prompt size and max output tokens. Phase 2: Support a dedicated local classifier endpoint. Good candidates: * Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B-Instruct * Qwen3-0.6B * Llama 3.2 1B * Gemma 3 1B * SmolLM2 360M / 1.7B The router should be compatible with Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio, or any OpenAI-compatible local endpoint. Phase 3: Let the router choose model tier for advisor calls: * `light_advisor` → local model * `architecture` / `safety_sensitive` → stronger local or hosted SOTA * `question` → user-configured advisor model * `repo_memory` → retrieve first, then maybe advisor Do not fine-tune the classifier until we have real routing logs and eval data. ## Logging and evals Every policy decision should be logged with enough information to debug routing quality: ```ts interface AdvisorPolicyLog { timestamp: string; promptHash: string; intent: AdvisorIntent; preflight: PreflightPolicy; review: ReviewPolicy; confidence: number; reason: string; safety: boolean; repoMemory: boolean; delegate?: string[]; fallbackUsed: boolean; } ``` Add a small eval file: `evals/advisor-router.jsonl` Example cases: ```jsonl {"input":"continue","expected":{"intent":"quick_edit","preflight":"off"}} {"input":"show me the diff","expected":{"intent":"quick_edit","preflight":"off"}} {"input":"fix the lint errors","expected":{"intent":"implementation","preflight":"light"}} {"input":"why is this test failing?","expected":{"intent":"debug","review":"error_only"}} {"input":"review this implementation","expected":{"intent":"review","review":"strict"}} {"input":"should we rewrite this around plugins?","expected":{"intent":"architecture","preflight":"full","review":"strict"}} {"input":"upgrade all dependencies to latest","expected":{"intent":"architecture","preflight":"full","review":"strict"}} {"input":"delete old migration files","expected":{"intent":"safety_sensitive","safety":true}} {"input":"change the auth token handling","expected":{"intent":"safety_sensitive","safety":true}} {"input":"why did this repo choose this config layout?","expected":{"intent":"repo_memory","repoMemory":true}} {"input":"ask advisor whether this API shape is sane","expected":{"intent":"question","preflight":"direct","directAdvisor":true}} ``` ## Acceptance criteria * Existing advisor config continues to work. * `mode: off` disables router effects. * `mode: manual` prevents automatic preflight/direct advisor calls. * `review: off` disables post-review even if router requests it. * `review: strict` strengthens non-off review policies. * Quick edits can skip advisor preflight. * Architecture/refactor/tradeoff prompts receive full preflight. * Debug prompts prefer error-focused review. * Review prompts run strict review without unnecessary preflight. * Direct strategic questions can route to advisor directly. * Safety-sensitive prompts are detected by deterministic override. * Classifier failure falls back to conservative light behavior. * Last policy is stored and consumed by post-review hooks. * Routing decisions are logged. * A small eval set exists for router behavior. * No dedicated local model is required for MVP, but the design leaves room for it. ## Non-goals for first PR * No fine-tuning. * No full cross-package orchestration bus. * No mandatory local model install. * No complex parallel side-channel advisor UX. * No hard latency target beyond “cheap enough not to feel annoying.” ## Follow-up opportunities * Dedicated local tiny classifier model. * Grammar-constrained JSON output. * User commands to force routing: * `/advisor route off` * `/advisor route light` * `/advisor route full` * `/advisor route direct` * UI/debug line: * `Advisor: skipped preflight for quick_edit, confidence 0.92` * Periodic eval generation from real routing logs. * Optional delegation integration with `pi-guardrails`, `pi-brain`, and `pi-repo-arch`. ``` My meta-verdict: **this ticket becomes much stronger if it is about adaptive advisor policy, not “LLM gatekeeper.”** Grok’s “delegate” addition is useful, but I’d keep it as logged metadata first. The first PR should make the current advisor feel smarter without turning Pi into a giant orchestration framework overnight. ```
